In the dimly lit conference room, the candidate sits across from the hiring manager, the air thick with tension. The interview is a formality, a chance to prove themselves to the company and secure a job in their field. But as the man starts to talk, the candidate can feel their obsession creeping in. They cannot let go of the idea that they are the best candidate for the job, and their mind races with all the reasons why they are right.

The room was dimly lit, casting eerie shadows on the walls and casting a coldness that made her skin tingle. She felt a sense of foreboding wash over her, as though the universe was conspiring against her, ready to throw a monkey wrench into her plans.
Her mind raced as she tried to come up with a solution to the interview. But the words kept escaping her mouth, and she found herself repeating them in her head, over and over again. "I'm not an expert in this field," she said, trying to mask her embarrassment.

As the interview began, the candidate's confidence grew, and they seemed to have everything under control. But as soon as the questions began, a wave of self-doubt washed over them.
The interviewer scanned their resume, pausing briefly at each section before continuing on to the next. The candidate felt their heart pounding in their chest, their palms sweating as they scanned the room, searching for a sign of encouragement.
But then, the interviewer turned to them and said, "Excuse me, but I've noticed that you've been avoiding answering some of these questions."
